title: Meridian — A Navigation OS
sub: Redesigning spatial awareness for the next generation of autonomous systems
content:
Overview

Meridian is a comprehensive navigation operating system built for environments where GPS signals are absent or unreliable — underground transit, dense urban canyons, and indoor industrial complexes. The project spanned three years of research, prototyping, and deployment across twelve cities.

The Problem

Existing navigation systems fail catastrophically in GPS-denied environments. Transit operators, emergency responders, and logistics teams rely on outdated paper maps or expensive proprietary solutions with poor accuracy. Meridian set out to solve this with a hybrid sensor-fusion approach.

Research Phase

We conducted contextual interviews with 84 transit operators across six countries, observing how they navigate signal loss and how errors compound over time. The core insight: people needed confidence signals as much as accurate data.

Design Decisions

The interface prioritises three things: ambient awareness, quick correction, and graceful degradation. When sensor confidence drops below a threshold, the UI shifts into a distinctive low-confidence mode — the design communicates uncertainty rather than hiding it.

Technical Architecture

Meridian fuses data from inertial measurement units, barometric pressure sensors, WiFi fingerprinting, and machine-learned dead reckoning. The entire stack runs on-device, processing at 120Hz with under 4ms latency.

Outcome

Deployed in three metro systems. Achieved sub-metre accuracy in 94% of tested corridors. Average operator trust score: 8.7/10 — up from 3.2 with previous systems.

title: Forma — Material Intelligence
sub: An AI-assisted material selection tool for sustainable architecture
content:
Background

Architecture firms spend an average of 340 hours per project on material research and specification. Forma reduces this to under 40 hours while simultaneously improving sustainability outcomes and cost predictability.

Core Challenge

Material selection involves thousands of interdependent variables: structural performance, embodied carbon, cost trajectories, lead times, aesthetic coherence, and regulatory compliance. No single expert can hold all of this in their head. Forma makes it possible.

Intelligence Layer

The underlying model was trained on 2.3 million material specifications, 180,000 completed projects, and real-time pricing from 600 suppliers. It understands context — a coastal building faces different material constraints than an inland one, even if the programs are identical.

Interface Design

Rather than surfacing a list of options, Forma surfaces a field of possibilities — a spatial canvas where materials cluster by compatibility, sustainability profile, and cost band. Architects explore by zooming, filtering, and comparing, not by navigating menus.

Collaboration Features

A shared project space allows structural engineers, interior designers, and clients to annotate the material field together. Decisions are logged with reasoning, creating a searchable specification history that survives personnel changes.

Impact

Used by 340 architecture firms in 28 countries. Average embodied carbon reduction: 23% compared to traditional selection methods. Average specification time reduction: 88%.
